speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Laura Brackett Albright took place at her home on Potlatch Creek below

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: the said, now Clarence needs, and his mother couldn't talk English and his father just a little bit. See, this is a long time ago, you know, the Indians lived up here on there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, would you like me to give him a special reading lesson?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: They were concerned with the English language. I said, if he can stay fifteen minutes after school, I will do some special work with Clarence.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so the mother muttered something and turned around and went in the house, but the father looked at me and he said, just what would you do?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And I said, I think that Clarence's trouble is in identifying the words. He looks at those words and I said, now I suppose that his eyes are right, but I said, it's a slow comprehension. It's a slow understanding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know, do you think you know what I'm talking about?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And he thought a little bit and he said, if I teach you Indian language, you be slow. And I said, yes, I would be slow because I would have to learn what the words meant. And I said, this is what Clarence has. He says, okay, you do him. And so then I said, now it's liable to get dark and in the winter time it's getting darker. Now this was just before Thanksgiving.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, could I bring him home from school? And he said, how you bring him?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And I said, well, I'd have to walk like Clarence does. No, he says, I come get him horseback. I said, okay, then you'll be down here by 4 .30 and then we'll be ready. And Clarence, I don't think I ever really taught him anything, but he just loved that special attention because you see, he had been missing it. And so he would stay 15 minutes after school every day. And then two of my white kids felt that I was being unfair and they asked to stay too, but they were fifth graders. Clarence was supposed to be a second grader. He was 10 years old in the second year, in the second grade. You see that show, they just didn't do anything for him. And so the other kids stayed and they stayed because when they went home, their father sent them out to Buckwood and all this sort of thing. And they didn't have a chance to study. So they stayed and studied. And then I gave them some reading too because they were used to, you know, they look at this word and say every, and then they looked at the next one, four weeks. And each word, you see, instead of seeing a sentence or seeing, it was just each one. So then I started with the placards with two or three words.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like I said, what did you do this morning when you went out to milk the cows?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Shovelled snow. I said, okay, Neville and shoveled so. Neville and shoveled snow. Neville and shoveled snow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, how'd you know you did?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, he says, anybody knows how to shovel snow. I said, okay, that's what those words mean. Neville and, which was the boy shoveled snow. Oh, that's what it's all about. I mean, it's amazing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And of course, that's why I love to teach in the first, are you recording that?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yeah, I am now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The whole thing is to get some of the stories down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, was it hard on the Mox Mox boy in terms of the other kids being 10 years old and at the low level of 30 years?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: No, in the small rural school, and this was a small one, there was only 20 pupils there. And I had six grades. I didn't have the fourth grade and I didn't have the seventh grade. I put the seventh and eighth graders together. I only had two. And I found that the reading and the spelling could be correlated very well. And in the small school, I didn't have any, I didn't have that trouble, thank goodness. They, after I got Dallas kind of calmed down a little bit while it was fine. But his older sister, this youngster that was just 13 years old, was keeping house for her father and a bedridden grandmother and the four brothers and sisters in that home and coming to my school. And one of the things that Clarence likes to do is to sing. And so he would come early so that I could play the organ and sing. I mean, this is what you call a rap work these days, I suppose, because I had to earn the respect and the liking and whatever of these youngsters because I don't think any teacher can teach youngsters that doesn't have their respect and their liking. And in the rural schools, we were much closer to our pupils because we were there all the time. It wasn't like going to a class for this and that and the other. And so I liked it. This was the second school that I taught and was really an easy school in a way because I had to learn about the Indians. Some of the gross -closed children, and I don't like to call names, also were handicapped because their folks came from Virginia and for generation upon generation they had intermarried because they would not marry out of their family and out of their class. And so some of those youngsters were retarded and were handicapped. And so I had this problem too of could I see that they had done some things. One of those girls now has caught up with me. She's not only a great grandmother but one of my great friends and lives in the orchards. Helen Gross -Closed was Helen Stingman those days. Her father was now on the clerk at school board.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that reflect the parents and the families or was it the kids themselves dividing themselves up?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: It was just one of those things. In the old days the teacher was supposed to be the center of the school which was always the center of the community. Every community exercise, and I use this from voting to parties to what have you, was in the school and the teacher was expected to do this. A literary every Friday night. Finally it became so heavy that I said now rather just have a sketchy one, let's have one once a month or at the most twice a month and then all of you take part. If some of you want to debate, oh dear don't get me started on debates. By doing it this way and by reaching out into the community I had a wonderful opportunity. That's when I learned that Sam Waters which was a young Indian man about 23, 24 played the piano beautifully and sang on a, he could go way up in G and sing soprano and go way down to the bass and sing the bass down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I had made the remark, I said well I am sitting here playing the organ with my back to everybody, how can I preside and literate and do this? And they said, doesn't anyone else in the community play?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yeah, there was Sam so I went over and Sam now you see is a retired fellow that lives down here in Spalding, we've been friends all these years. Then we had the groups, the grownups and Levi Grossclose who is now dead and was Dallas' father and some father of the other youngsters wanted to debate and he was a lawyer in Virginia when he came out here. And so of course he wanted to debate. So my goodness we had hot and heavy debates and I learned about debating listening to community debates. I mean this is what we did. And then Sam would come and help play and we would sing the patriotic songs and if you are up against it you can do an awful lot of things if you have to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have party games too?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yes, but not the paring off kind because there was too much, we had little bitty youngsters and great grandpa and grandmas you know and some of them would want to sit and look and listen and be entertained. And so when we had some of the party games I would select certain leaders to be. For instance, well let's see, I wonder some of them we did in the summertime of course while we had the singing games like Skip to Mooloo, My Darling out there. In the schoolhouse it wasn't possible because we didn't have a special room and so we had to have like button button who's got the button and then I would make it like make it over like oh some of the others and I'd have some of the little youngsters pass the button and then we'd guess who had the button and everybody would laugh and then we'd get up and play Skip to Mooloo and sing the songs and then I'd have a contest who could make up the best verse and you'd be surprised what would come out of those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The only thing that I laid down the law I said it's got to be decent, we've got the kids here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would adults as well as the kids take part in this?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This question of what you're talking about the role that the teacher took in the community is very interesting. I'm curious to know more about this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You consider this to be more or less the position of intellectual leadership in the community would that be fair to say?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yes, she was expected to as a teacher she was expected to be intellectual she was expected to sometimes have an opinion that would be acceptable in the community. Now any teacher man or woman and many times the men were teaching out there if they wanted to be controversial all they had to do was just go against the common culture of the community and they were just simply ostracized that's all there was to it. They wouldn't be effective as teachers how could they if they didn't have the respect of the people and the children and what have you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that how would that kind of situation come about where a teacher would wind up going against the grain of the community?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't like to put this in the record. Don't use any name then but I'm curious to know how a teacher would defend the community in such a way that he was no longer effective. That's the important thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be getting beyond what was acceptable to the people?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well there's two things a teacher can be so very intellectual and so very high and mighty that she and I will say she because I was a teacher would not listen to her school board for instance because they were plain men and they didn't use good grammar and she would reflect this in her dealings with them and when you get a school board and a community and she usually lived in the community and boarded with some of them and any family will do things that is strange to a girl that goes in for instance. I mustn't tell this. I mustn't tell it because everybody knows me and they know my history.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will you turn it off now? Is there a way we can?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Working for his uncle George in his uncle George Albright's bicycle shop that turned into a car mechanics and Dr. Rickman and some of these that were the Owens. Oh yeah you mentioned that to me that he worked for him. He drove for him and then he taught himself the mechanics of this and did fine until his health broke because and then and so I'd heard all about Raleigh Albright up there when I was in but I never really met him till I come down here and this Aunt Susan's home was just around the bend up here and that White House the Johnson house was the Albright house you see that's where Raleigh was in at home. So he talked his mother into taking me in because he was afraid I was going to go home. I've been here ever since 59 years this fall. Well okay so we talked about about the literary things and just thoroughly enjoy it and her husband who was the clerk of the board felt that he was quite the king's man and he would lead many of the debates I mean in the community. And I'm sorry in a way that many of these rural schools are passing because the youngsters in the lower grades learned from the youngsters in the upper grades how do you carry on 75 classes a day in seven grades with from five to six subjects in each grade and do it in six hours or four hours or eight hours you see. And we didn't have a lunch the youngsters would bring their own lunches but I made it my business to see that there was hot water and then cocoa and things that we would have we didn't actually have school lunches but we did have this because the children had to eat and some of the youngsters didn't have decent things they would bring raw carrots and just eat nothing but raw carrots. Well when it's cold and some of those youngsters didn't have clothing like they should have had and so we had to sometimes I would take extra food because when I stayed at Mrs. Albright's why she realized the situation and in fact she held herself quite aloof from the rest of them because she was poor white trash from Virginia just wasn't up her alley and she was going to make her family American or know the reason why and I already knew I was American I didn't have to worry about making myself American.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She called it outside culture is that what she called it? What do you think she meant by that?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well I think she meant the she had struggled up through her 16th year and finally got through the eighth grade and that's as high as she could ever gone and she would have long she would have loved to gone on to high school it wasn't possible and so she married and then had this large family and she was just so thoroughly thrilled and in the singing for instance I'd say well let's let's how about singing a part can you sing a part and she couldn't read a note you know and she said if you will if you'll learn it to me I can and so I would sit down and we would pick out the and I'd make like say the alto in just an alto tune and she learned that tune and she could sing it and she didn't know I mean this sort of thing that was good for her she wanted to do this that's my telephone thing. The river halfway up that hill over there and we were in the shack you haven't seen the picture of the little shack. I did I looked at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yeah. And I had to get some lunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you get for lunch? I thought I had some napkins but I guess I'll have to get some others and I thought oh what will I give that phone for lunch?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Gee I just don't know what to do and I was out there and it was so hard to do anything with it and I'm going to give you that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah how about that?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so I went down to the river to get a pail of water and I saw these band dandelion buds. The dandelions were just it was just about this time of the year it was a little earlier and they were coming up and they were just great dandelions in the grass you know like this and there was those buds and I thought well gee I don't have any lettuce but if I had I can get some of those I'll I'll I'll make a hot salad. So I had some old dried hard home cured bacon that was so salty that we couldn't eat it and for breakfast anymore very much we were tired of it we had to all witter. So I cooked up that and I had some homemade vinegar and I got the vinegar in it and I picked two cups of dandelion buds and a cup full of the tender leaves that I cut up and put in there. Then I washed them and put them into a dish and I went home and I cut up this hard bacon that was so hard that nobody could eat it very well but you see I didn't have to solve the stuff and I decided that the vinegar was too harsh so I put a little water in it and then I had a lot of eggs thank goodness and so I broke three eggs in there and scrambled them all up and thickened it a little bit and put it over those and I made some homemade biscuits and when that man came I gave him this hot salad this is dandelion bud salad and I hope that you'll like it Mr. Shell appealed and he said well I think I will usually my mother used to cook dandelion greens and I said well this is not the boiled greens this is a hot salad and he just ate them and he said my goodness that's wonderful he said where did you get that recipe and I said oh it's been an old family secret for a long time and he ate those hot bacon powder biscuits and and that dandelion salad with the meat and that's all he had oh yes he did too I had some canned pears but I'll never forget that because I and so the family has eaten dandelion bud salads ever since when you can go out and buy and pick enough and you'd be surprised this little lawn out here I can pick a couple of those in dandelion season right easily then another thing that excellent yes isn't that good really tasty very the blackberries that they they're the Himalaya blackberries that the gross closes brought from Virginia long long ago and you know how they have taken over the countryside these berry bushes are clear up to me I they're all around spalling they're all over and they started from the the gross close farm which is by the Johnson house it's up here as you as you go to before you get to Julietta that the White House where the Johnson's live used to be the old Albright place and the there is no longer just around the bend on this steep bank was where this answers and gross close house was and the gross closes and there were two brothers that came Levi gross close was the lawyer that was a young fellow that came from Virginia and then Uncle Jim was his uncle that came and then there were brothers and that's where the gross closes came so long ago from there and they've spread out over the state since then and they brought these Himalaya blackberries with them and the old walnut tree that's on the left hand as you go back it's it's an old old tree Bessie gross close best insurance Barry she was married planted that and all of the walnut trees in the country and I've come from that we planted a lot of them out here the children are planting them again so the walnut trees came from this is the size yes they brought the look out there now can you see that magpie that's on the on the roof there there he just flew yeah well I have to feed my cats on the roof to keep the dogs from getting him and so now the magpies you're eating the cat food I have to feed him after night when the magpies go to bed and it makes me feel real badly because after all magpies have babies that have to feed too. That's a,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: that's very you know there aren't many people around I've known who come from the south like like yeah yeah it's interesting to me that they brought some of those things out with them and that they've kind of taken root and done so well. Yes, that's right.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And Macy Nye of Julietta you've met her you see her father was the undertaker and old Uncle Joe Aunt Laura was a very fine real nice person Uncle Joe was nice too but he was right on the borderline and and Macy, Macy's father gave the as you know the land for the church there and helped build the church a long time ago and that was the the other side of the picture you see they were so dedicated Christian people there but at the same time handicapped by the lack of education they had although he was a businessman and a Sharpie I mean he was an an embalmer and he had graduated from this embalming school they didn't have quite so much in those days they do now but he was our undertaker up there until he passed away and had a store there not not the grocery store but the furniture store because that fit in with his embalming business you see.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, I've noticed in talking to people old -timers I find that a surprising number of the women that I talked to did teach at one time. Yes, I think that this was really true because the teachers would come into a community and they were always outsiders and they were well educated naturally and they were usually responsible persons and you couldn't blame those young men for trying to marry them if they could and this and this this is true this is this is this is true. I when I came down here of course my story is a little different too because I was engaged time to a doctor and had looked forward to perhaps being a doctor's wife and feeling a little bit put upon because I wasn't sure I was ready to be stayed I thought a doctor's wife had to be very dignified and very stayed and I was such a happy -go -lucky and I had this wonderful horse that I wanted to be and I wanted to be myself after all and so I broke my engagement and fell in love with Raleigh and I so I'm here ever since. But you know. And this this is the story of a great many teachers coming into a small community and I I think that you'll run into this more and more I'm you've already spoken about it. I have seen it you know and what I've been thinking about that lately and and thinking that that it must have been for most women the best opportunity that that presented itself to become highly active in in the early communities now this may be maybe I'm not correct but I have had that impression because because the role was there and I think that maybe I I would like to say it this way the these girls these young women that would come into the community mostly had been to college and had had more opportunities than those on the local level and so of course they they represented something that the local people were happy to have for most part it wasn't always true sometimes teachers we get off on the wrong foot and then they say oh she's a stuck up thing and I can't be bothered with her and so on and this is these are the unhappy tales Sam because many teachers couldn't bear the responsibility of the and you go into a school now when I taught at lookout I had 78 pupils and some of those big boys were as old as I was and I had gone to school with some of those boys and how do you how do you keep discipline and how do you say now look this isn't the right thing Sam you're not doing it right because you don't know your multiplication tables now you learn your multiplication tables and you have to watch you have to watch it I did two things with a couple as I thinking back it's been so long ago Sam but one of the boys that went to my school wanted to be an auctioneer and I didn't know the auctioneers that I knew at the time well they just started out and were auctioneers and they worked up a pattern it didn't make any difference if they put a few cuss words in and what have you well that was accepted but this this youngster had brains and I had to find out and I made it my business to find out where there would be a school where could he go and learn to really be an auctioneer and I did it through the libraries now this was one of the things you haven't touched on this Sam either and the rural schools had the opportunity to build a fine library and there was a certain percentage of taxes that went into this that was a library levy and if the teacher did right by the community that library was open to the people and so I wrote to the to the county library in this case and I said where can I get material for an auction school and I got all kinds of things back and then this lad went on and earned his spurs and is down south now just to go he went back to his people and he's built up a big auctioneering thing there another one of the boys wanted to be a veterinarian and I didn't know anything about it except how we could and I went through the same procedure made it my business to find out to help this youngster and his folks when they they got the brochures from the school his folks couldn't possibly have sent him and so I worked with this youngster and he had no typewriter and he wanted to learn to type and I said well I'm not sure how good you are Dirk but I said maybe you could get some some work addressing envelopes or something I said let's look into it well what he finally got was a an assistant janitorship in a city school you see because he'd had the experience there and so the teachers role in the old days as particularly and I expect it's true yet in the rural schools that we had this this this outside culture so -called was outside really context they wanted to know how to do see and if the teacher was was as responsible as most of them were they would find out how to do this you see and so library you're quite right I don't know about libraries oh you hadn't thought well no what was that situation well there was always a eat every rural school had a library and there was a special levy a certain amount of the percentage of the tax that was allowable and our little pilot rock up here was very very fortunate because we had the railroad you see it wasn't we didn't have people that would vote against the library because well they felt the Union Pacific and the Northern Pacific was paying an awful lot of taxes there anyway which was true and so I could spend the limit on this and one year I know I spent a hundred and eighty five dollars on the books to to get them and then many of them wanted just novels well I couldn't see this you see and so I we had a PTA oh my goodness and I talked to them and I said now I'm getting ready to make this order for four books but we must have school books and I said we need reading material badly that will fit into our lower grades but I said I am perfectly willing to to get at least four books for the general public if you will tell me which ones you want and some of them opted for novels and then others wanted some of the university books I mean one on soils I remember one of the young farmers wanted a book on soils and I said well we can buy it but it will be all of seven dollars and in those days this was just to take up a lot of the money and I said I think that maybe I can borrow and I'll see if I can borrow from a library somewhere and see if somebody else may have it and I'll make it my business to do so I didn't get the one he wanted but I got some others and I mean this is what you did and the teacher had to be the librarian naturally what would you say roughly would be the composition of the library what would its makeup be some do -it -yourself pamphlets and I got these and the classics now some like this Levi Gross close that I talked about he felt real badly that I wasn't putting on Shakespeare plays well you can't put on Shakespeare plays very well with it the kind of youngsters I had and do a decent job that would do something and so I wrote to and my County superintendent was Miss Redfield I wrote to Miss Redfield and I said I have had a call for some special how to do it books and they weren't as popular in those days as they are now I said is there any place where I can get them and because Ethel Redfield had some southern people in her background she says let me write and she wrote to Arkansas and Missouri as I remember just right off the bat and they had some books that were put out by a composition a composition of authors in the Mississippi Valley that was above greening as I understand it and I borrowed those books and they sent them out here on her miss Redfield said that Miss Brackett will be responsible for them and we borrowed them for six months and they practically wore them out do it yourself do what you so yeah the first composting was was one of the things there and our well let me go back a little bit the the farmers were lazy and they wanted their winter corrals on the creek bank and there was too many people that was getting water that had come from that Creek and to shallow wells and what have you and so the first thing I did was to say it look now maybe some of these things that are happening and there was things of Sam like st. Vitus dance I never heard of st. Vitus dance and I I didn't know what it was and I found out it was malnutrition that causes st. Vitus dance and it was because of polluted water that we were getting and this is when I started to do something about water how do you how do you get water well that's when we heard I learned about drilling through the you get down to the hard pan when you're digging a well with a shovel and that's it but with the drills that they commenced to have in about that time I then we got this good well up here with that through three hard pans before we got to good water and this sort of thing you see and if we could have taught these people and some of the farmers did they they could realize that for their own safety they should have a well that was free of the barnyard and so how to do it yourself and then the other one was weaving now the gross closes that came from Virginia knew about flax and weaving and about yarn and knitting and about spinning the yarn they had would my husband's people had sheep at the time and they'd go up and get that wool and gee there was burrs in it this these terrible burrs that crinkle all up in the wool and you can't get it out without tearing it all to pieces and then you've got those spurs in the yarn so we had to learn about weeds and if you want to protect your crops which happened to be wool off a sheep and and the mutton then you then you get rid of the weeds so you don't have cockle burrs and then I remember another thing that bothered me a little bit when I was teaching up here was the unsanitary conditions with milk they would go out and milk their cows and then forget to keep the milk clean you know and there's nothing as dirty as dirty milk if you don't I mean so I sent to the university they had the the bows had the dairy up there you know on the dairy cows and they these people had some good cows and I said it's a shame to just get that milk and then you know well two or three of the people kind of felt badly so I went back to my miss Redfield again and I said can I have some advice about how to have milk tested for bacterial count and for things like this and I said now we've got the state by the stance and I'm calling in the the county doctor and I thought that that as a county school mom I had the right to to ask him and it was dr. Ally and bless his heart he came but a county doctor is only to dr. County patients but because the school teacher yelled out here and I needed help and I told him about the milk situation and about the water situation and I said how can these youngsters possibly grow up with all these handicaps what can we do about it would you come out and would you help me and so he came but sometimes it pays to be ignorant Sam but the county the counter outside culture to get back to this again this is what it was was mostly the know -how and the willing to find out and to know now I didn't know all this where to get but you see I had people I could reach you could rely on miss Redfield and then because I had been a summer school and the normal I didn't always get good response from them because they had their own programs and their own work but the superintendent of the high school the Lewis and high school was still around when I was teaching that I had been there in my sophomore year and I had such a deep respect for this person that I wrote and I said if you were out in the country school and this wasn't up here it was at my first school in Jackson Canyon which was this is bad or worse than this how would you go about how could I go about finding out who to write to and I don't know how many hours professor who then spent but working for me and telling me this this is what these people great this is what they wanted to know this is what they expected of a teacher to have these outside contacts now I was not too musical but we up here one year we had a teacher that was real musical and she could play anything from a sweet potato to a hot potato and the kids just loved it and the rhythm bands came in see I didn't have sense enough to have a rhythm band because they know anything about it I thought that they had to whistle or sing or play an accordion or a mouth harp or something like this you see and then we tapped our toes and I could have made a drum if I'd have been around long enough at my school was only seven months long you must understand well take Jackson Canyon were the people there that that needed a certain amount of assistance that needed to get some more progressive ideas about how things should be done we were was the cause there that the with people I mean why did they have that that lack of knowledge in their background is what I'm curious to know were they were they southerners were they from from the hill country what was the story there in Jack's Canyon it was a as I think about it they there was some very fine farmers there but there was also what we would call in those days hillbillies and they never expected to get beyond the eighth grade and as soon as they could cipher the boys would go into the fields to to earn a living I mean it was one of these things they weren't interested in it and until I the new thing in the fields at that time was alfalfa believe it or not it was it was a new crop and they turned their cows into alfalfa they were used to it and it killed them and they figured that it was poison but you see it was just because this and so I said well there must be some reason that alfalfa is coming to the front and I and they said well does your father have this alfalfa and I said no he doesn't he has Timothy and clover because he was raised in Maine and and the crop he grew up with was Timothy and clover but I said the Timothy you see the clover will also cause the same thing that you alfalfa alfalfa is doing but he puts Timothy with it I said why don't you write to the university because in those days that's where we got our we didn't all have the county agents you see in those days long ago so that's this would be well I'm 78 and I was 18 at the time so you can see it 60 years ago and it was just starting out and so they would find out and silo they were they
